Locking

Locking

Uniface offers three locking strategies and various ProcScript instructions for implementing transactions.

The locking strategy determines when a lock is acquired:

  • Optimistic locking—Uniface escalates a database lock at the moment the modified data is written to the database
  • Cautious locking—a database lock is set at the moment the data is modified in a Uniface form, service, or report
  • Paranoid locking—the locks are acquired when the data is read from the database

Selecting the appropriate locking strategy is a modeling issue and depends on the situation. The strategy has a significant impact on performance.

Locks are released by issuing either a commit or rollback ProcScript statement or by exiting the application. The rollback statement makes it possible to return to the consistent state at the start of the transaction, for example, if an error has occurred during the transaction. Each DBMS or file system has its own way of processing lock requests issued by Uniface. For more information, see the specific connector documentation or the relevant database documentation.

Relational database systems have the ability to lock data at different levels:

  • RDBMS level locks—The entire RDBMS is locked, resulting in a single user environment. Locking at a lower level is not needed in a single user environment. RDBMS locking can be used in batch processes and can be activated when no other users are working on the system (for example, overnight or during the weekends). It is recommended that you disable the locking mechanism for such processes by leaving the LOCK triggers empty, so Uniface does not execute a lock, and, as a result, performance is improved.
  • Table level locks—the database locks the entire table (entity). This can be useful if a table is accessed by a single user at a time.
  • Block level locks—part of the table is locked (a set of occurrences) at a time. This is sometimes called page-level locking.
  • Row level locks—one row (occurrence) is locked at a time.
  • Field level locks—one field (attribute) is locked at a time.
